examples.title: Fibonacci - Memoization (Top-Down)
examples.description: Top-down dynamic programming approach using recursion with memoization
examples.explanation: Uses dictionary to cache computed Fibonacci values. Each subproblem is solved only once, reducing time from exponential to linear.

examples.title: Fibonacci - Tabulation (Bottom-Up)
examples.description: Bottom-up dynamic programming approach using iteration
examples.explanation: Builds solution from base cases iteratively. The optimized version uses only two variables to reduce space complexity to O(1).

examples.title: 0/1 Knapsack Problem
examples.explanation: Creates a 2D table where dp[i][w] represents maximum value using first i items with capacity w. Backtracking finds which items were selected.

examples.title: Longest Common Subsequence
examples.description: DP solution to find LCS length and reconstruct the sequence
examples.explanation: Builds DP table where each cell contains LCS length. If characters match, extends diagonally; otherwise takes maximum from left or top cell. Backtracking reconstructs the actual LCS.

examples.title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
examples.description: Two approaches: O(n²) DP and O(n log n) binary search
examples.explanation: DP approach compares each element with all previous elements. Binary search approach maintains a 'tail' array representing smallest ending element for each length, achieving O(n log n).

examples.title: Matrix Chain Multiplication
examples.description: DP solution to find minimum scalar multiplications
examples.code:
def matrix_chain_order(dims):
    n = len(dims) - 1  # Number of matrices
    dp = [[0] * n for _ in range(n)]
    
    for length in range(2, n + 1):  # chain length
        for i in range(n - length + 1):
            j = i + length - 1
            dp[i][j] = float('inf')
            
            for k in range(i, j):
                cost = (dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + 
                        dims[i] * dims[k+1] * dims[j+1])
                dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j], cost)
    
    return dp[0][n-1]

# dims = [d0, d1, d2, ..., dn] where matrix i is di-1 x di
# For A:10x30, B:30x5, C:5x60 → dims=[10,30,5,60]
# Result: minimum scalar multiplications

# Time: O(n³), Space: O(n²)
examples.explanation: Tries all possible split points to find minimum multiplication cost. The cost formula accounts for multiplying two result matrices after optimal parenthesization of subchains.
